automation_impact.low: Read-only analysis or documentation in normal use.
automation_impact.medium: Can label, comment, fail checks, publish reports, or modify local files.
automation_impact.high: Can close, lock, delete, block, limit interactions, or change repository settings.

methodology.important: Impact describes documented capability, not product quality or maliciousness. Configuration can reduce or increase actual risk.

audits.deployment: Hosted service, CLI, web UI, Docker, or self-hosted source
audits.default_mode: Dry-run; strong actions are opt-in
audits.access: GitHub token; optional model endpoint and API key
audits.limitations: AI classification is probabilistic. Review evidence and planned actions in dry-run before apply mode. GitHub does not detect the license automatically, but the repository LICENSE file contains Apache-2.0 text.

audits.default_mode: Enforcement; the documented quick start closes a PR after the failure threshold
audits.access: Recommended: contents read, issues read, pull requests write
audits.limitations: Heuristics include account and contribution-history signals, so legitimate newcomers can be flagged. Set close-pr and lock-pr to false during evaluation.

audits.default_mode: Applies moderation output unless dry-run is enabled
audits.access: GitHub token plus models read and write access needed for configured moderation actions
audits.limitations: Repository content is sent to a model endpoint and model judgments can be wrong. Its built-in AI-authorship prompt is not proof of authorship; disable it for enforcement. GitHub Models paid usage may apply after free limits.

audits.default_mode: Model-driven moderation using project guidelines
audits.access: GitHub token, models read, and write permissions for enabled actions
audits.limitations: A language model interprets community standards; ambiguous or multilingual content needs human review and an appeal path.

audits.default_mode: Posts a structured assessment and applies configured labels
data_boundaries: GitHub Models or a compatible endpoint
audits.access: GitHub token, models read, issues write, and contents read for prompt files
audits.limitations: The assessment is model-generated, not factual proof. Prompt changes and model changes can alter classification behavior.

audits.default_mode: Allows, reviews, or denies based on trust tier and a multi-signal score
audits.access: GitHub token with pull-request write access; optional registry API key
audits.limitations: History-based scoring and optional AI co-author rejection can exclude legitimate contributors. Low-confidence cases are forced to review, but maintainers should still test thresholds.

audits.deployment: GitHub Actions and a repository trust file
audits.default_mode: Explicit admission based on project-defined vouches and denouncements
audits.access: Depends on action; write permission is required for close or lock behavior
audits.limitations: Explicit trust can protect attention but can also create a closed or biased contributor network. Define transparent nomination and appeal rules.

audits.deployment: GitHub Action, CLI, Python package, and MCP server
audits.default_mode: Posts a trust-score comment; fail-on-low is false by default
data_boundaries: Python package registry during Action installation
audits.access: GitHub token; pull-request write for comments and optional checks write
audits.limitations: Past merge history is an imperfect proxy for trust and can disadvantage new or private contributors. The Action installs a version-ranged package from PyPI at runtime.

audits.default_mode: Adds or removes labels according to repository configuration
audits.access: Contents read and pull requests write
audits.limitations: Using pull_request_target is dangerous if the same workflow checks out or executes untrusted PR code. Labeling rules can misroute contributions.

audits.default_mode: Marks inactivity and closes after the configured grace period
audits.access: Issues and pull requests write; contents write only when deleting branches
audits.limitations: Inactivity does not mean low value. Exempt security, roadmap, accessibility, and confirmed-bug labels before enabling closure.

audits.default_mode: Locks closed threads after configured inactivity
audits.access: Write permission for each enabled issue, pull-request, or discussion scope
audits.limitations: Locking prevents late corrections and support follow-up. Exclusions and a clear path to open a new issue are important.

audits.default_mode: Immediately closes and locks configured new issues or pull requests
audits.access: Issues and/or pull requests write
audits.limitations: This is a blunt emergency control intended for repositories that do not accept submissions or for temporary incidents, not routine triage.

audits.default_mode: Reads repository activity and writes a Markdown report file
maximum_effects: write report file in the workflow workspace
audits.access: Issues, pull requests, or discussions read according to the search query; publishing requires a separate step and permission
audits.limitations: Response-time metrics do not measure answer quality or maintainer wellbeing. The official example uses a separate Action to publish the generated file as an issue; this tool does not publish it by itself.

audits.default_mode: Reports policy violations as issues in the quick start
data_boundaries: OpenSSF-operated service when using the public app
audits.access: Read access to most repository settings and contents; issues and checks write for reporting and block mode
audits.limitations: Organization-wide policy mistakes can affect many repositories. Start with opt-in scope and issue reporting before block enforcement.

audits.default_mode: Applies default-branch settings; pull-request changes are evaluated in dry-run
audits.access: Broad organization and repository administration permissions
audits.limitations: Configuration errors have organization-wide blast radius. Restrict managed repositories, protect the admin repository, and require review for settings changes.

audits.default_mode: Synchronizes settings from `.github/settings.yml`
audits.access: Repository administration permissions
audits.limitations: Anyone able to merge settings changes may gain or alter administrative access. Protect the configuration path with CODEOWNERS and required review.

audits.deployment: GitHub Action plus StepSecurity service; self-hosted runner support depends on tier
audits.default_mode: Audit network, file, and process activity
audits.access: Runs first in a CI job and observes process, file, and network activity
audits.limitations: The community and enterprise tiers differ, and monitoring data is presented through an external service. Review data handling and supported runner type before adoption.

audits.deployment: CLI, GitHub Action, REST API, and public dataset
audits.default_mode: Read-only heuristic assessment
data_boundaries: optional Scorecard API and public dataset
audits.access: Repository metadata read; additional permissions when uploading SARIF
audits.limitations: Scorecard documents that checks are heuristics with false positives and negatives. Weekly public results omit some checks and can be stale.

audits.deployment: Local CLI, package, or CI check
audits.default_mode: Read-only static analysis
data_boundaries: optional GitHub API for online audits
audits.access: Read access to workflow files; optional GitHub token
audits.limitations: Static analysis cannot prove a workflow is safe and may miss runtime or third-party Action behavior. Review findings in context.

audits.default_mode: Modifies files when pinning; supports a non-modifying verification mode
data_boundaries: GitHub API unless offline mode is used
audits.access: Filesystem write for fixes; optional GitHub token for release and tag lookup
audits.limitations: A pinned SHA limits tag movement but does not make third-party code trustworthy. Offline mode only checks SHA syntax.

audits.default_mode: Fails when configured vulnerability or license criteria are violated
audits.access: Contents read and pull requests read; optional pull requests write for comments
audits.limitations: Supported for public repositories and private repositories with GitHub Advanced Security. Results depend on dependency graph coverage and advisory data.

audits.default_mode: Scans and can verify discovered credentials
audits.access: Read access to scan targets; network access for verification
audits.limitations: Verification can send authentication requests to third-party services. Redact output, scope scans carefully, and treat findings as sensitive incident data.

audits.default_mode: Scans configured languages for suspicious patterns and can gate merge
audits.access: GitHub App private key, webhook secret, repository contents, and checks or status permissions
audits.limitations: Rules focus on dynamic execution and obfuscation in supported languages; passing does not prove a contribution is benign. Deployment requires operating a privileged GitHub App.

audits.default_mode: Read-only vulnerability and license scan
maximum_effects: modify dependency files in guided remediation
audits.access: Read access to dependency artifacts; filesystem and package-manager access for fix mode
audits.limitations: Coverage varies by ecosystem. Guided remediation can execute package-manager behavior from untrusted projects; do not run fix mode on untrusted code.

audits.deployment: CLI, pre-commit hook, and CI integrations
audits.default_mode: Read-only pattern-based secret detection
audits.access: Read access to files or Git history
audits.limitations: Pattern detection has false positives and false negatives. Keep output redacted and rotate any real credential rather than merely deleting it from the latest commit.

audits.default_mode: Documentation only
audits.access: None
audits.limitations: Policies differ by project and can change after collection. Confirm each linked source and seek legal advice for copyright, labor, or privacy questions.

audits.default_mode: Discussion and planned deliverables only
audits.access: None
audits.limitations: This is an open work item, not a finished best-practices document, standard, or OpenSSF certification.
